next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 3

Blue Hour Laundry breathed through forty open dryer doors.

The place sat on the canal market corner under a blue sign that buzzed hard enough to make the window glass tremble. Heat came out every time the front door opened, detergent heat and boiler heat and the sweeter animal note of damp cloth left too long in a basket. Under that there was Cordera itself, salt and fryer grease and tram brake dust. Mira stood in it with a milk crate of stolen-looking parts against her hip and watched Jo Villanueva snap a fitted sheet into obedience so sharply the woman folding beside her actually laughed and flinched at the same time.

Jo had a silver braid down her back and the hands of someone who had wrung water out of other people's lives for forty years. The skin over her knuckles looked sanded by soap. Her shoes were the practical black kind that promised no beauty and no mercy. She kept a cigarette tucked above one ear even though the city had not allowed indoor smoking in fifteen years and she had not been caught obeying anything in at least twenty.

"No," she said, before Tavi had even got the second crate through the door. "Absolutely not. You're not making my laundromat smell like cooked skin."

Tavi nudged the door shut with his heel. He had two salvage cases hanging from one hand and wore the same grin he used when the job was difficult enough to interest him and stupid enough to insult him. "Good evening to you too, Jo."

"You can have good evening when I stop hearing from three different women that some claims girl and my upstairs disappointment fixed a hotel...
open_hooks_to_carry: Show how claims language converts bodily anomaly into debt.
Give the poor client's economic reality enough weight that Mira's choice feels necessary rather than heroic.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...entered Celia's general floor code for provisional review, the one used when managers gave verbal instruction and did not want to come downstairs to sign for it themselves. The system accepted it. That was the ugliest part. The building was full of doors you could open if you already understood how much everyone wanted not to notice.

She printed the hold tag, looped it around the body's wrist, and watched the shelf light at station H-9 turn amber.

The change was small and quiet, and it felt irreversible enough.

LM-127B slid into the hold berth with the other delayed bodies, but it did not look like them to Mira now. It was not a spoiled lease or a fee event. It was something with a scar that had taken time, with sex written fresh across it and a drowning child preserved somewhere under the wash. A body printed to be empty had come back carrying too much life.

Outside the hold room the Claims Annex kept feeding the weekend. Someone laughed. Someone cried at a counter. A printer spat out invoices fast as breath. Mira stood with one hand over the hidden wafer in her cuff and understood, not fully and not yet in words she could defend, that this was bigger than one bad unit and one rich man's disposable night. You did not get healed history in fresh stock by accident. You did not get childhood terror in a dampened burner unless somebody, somewhere upstream, had decided the bargain line could be built out of whatever the city could not afford to protect.

She logged the hold, closed the screen, and went back out to the floor before she could talk herself into being sensible.
state_deltas_required: A poor client is about to be buried in damage fees for an injury CommonFrame will not acknowledge, and Mira knows official review will only make the debt more permanent.
Mira commits her first illegal repair and creates a working alliance with Tavi outside the claims system.
